critique.summary: The article presents a directory of over 135 open hardware devices that users can flash with their own firmware. It highlights a variety of development boards, modules, and single-board computers suitable for applications ranging from IoT and AI to industrial automation and smart home projects.
mainPoints: The directory features a wide range of open hardware devices including development boards, sensor modules, and single-board computers.
Devices support flashing custom firmware, enabling users to customize hardware for specific applications.
Products listed cover diverse use cases such as AI cameras, wireless sensor networks, industrial automation, smart home control, and edge AI.
Prices and technical specifications are provided for each device, facilitating informed purchasing decisions.
The directory emphasizes user control and flexibility in hardware and firmware choices.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately describes a curated list of open hardware devices available for purchase, including technical specifications and typical use cases. The information aligns with publicly available product details from manufacturers and distributors. However, the article does not cite external sources or provide multiple perspectives, as it primarily serves as a product directory rather than investigative journalism or analytical reporting.
considerations: The article does not discuss the potential challenges or limitations of using open hardware, such as compatibility issues or required technical expertise.
There is no mention of the security implications of flashing custom firmware on these devices.
The environmental impact or sustainability of the hardware products is not addressed.
Information about community support, software ecosystem maturity, or documentation quality for these devices is missing.
The article does not provide comparative analysis or user reviews to help evaluate device performance or reliability.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on a newly launched open hardware directory that lists devices flashable with custom firmware. While some users appreciate the concept and find the list a useful starting point, many criticize the site's user experience, AI-driven search functionality, and the accuracy of the open hardware claims.

comments.author: Karliss
comments.text:
The link was posted by project's author so probably should have been Show HN.

Feels more like AI slop list of "a bunch of hardware that you can buy from hobbyist electronic stores" which has no idea what it wants to be, shiny on surface but deeper you look less sense it makes. Not surprised, the company who made it (likely single person) describes itself as "We're crafting interesting tools to speed up software development using Artificial Intelligence."

Good chunk of that stuff is not open hardware by any definition -> neither the hardware design being open nor the firmware not even community written firmware for proprietary hardware.

If you ignore the poor description of the site is the parametric search at least good? The values in parameter dropdowns seem to be filled based on currently displayed items, that might be fine for narrowing down once you already made a search but for initial search it means you get random subset of available values. The fact that whole thing is non-categorized, random mix of mismatched type of hardware doesn't make the parametric search better. Good parametric search needs well curated and structured database of descriptions made by people who understand corresponding product category, otherwise it's garbage in garbage out.

Having to wait half a minute while AI is reticulating splines even when you used quite specific keywords isn't a good search experience either.

So if it's not a good list of open hardware, not a good list of hardware you can flash open firmware, not a good search for electronic components what is it good for? Only value I see is as a fuzzy set of links and ~~tags~~ for exploring a subset of related hardware topics.

Edit: not tags those are broken. #tags return error, other tags(uses cases) and other other tags(compatible firmware) in many cases returns only 1-2 results which doesn't even include the item where you clicked on tag even though there are a lot more items using it.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed critique of the site's shortcomings, including search and data curation issues, supported by examples and reasoning, making it highly accurate and insightful.

comments.author: haddonist
comments.text:
Looks good, but it'll probably take a while until it's anywhere close to the coverage of existing repositories:

https://templates.blakadder.com/ has almost 3,000 devices flashable onto Tasmota firmware.

For older Tuya devices there's https://github.com/tuya-cloudcutter/tuya-cloudcutter

OpenBeken https://github.com/openshwprojects/OpenBK7231T_App covers 800 of the newer generation Tuya devices.

And there's a large community adapting ESP32 devices onto https://esphome.io/
comments.analysis: The comment accurately contextualizes the directory's scope by comparing it to existing, well-known repositories, adding valuable perspective.

comments.author: anonymous_user9
comments.text:
AI slop. Most of the things on this list are not open hardware, and some of the items are completely proprietary. For example, the SLAMTEC RPLiDAR A1 [1]. This part doesn't even have user-upgradeable firmware.

[1] https://openhardware.directory/devices/slamtec-rplidar-a1
comments.analysis: This comment provides a concrete example to support the claim that the directory includes non-open hardware, reinforcing concerns about accuracy.

comments.author: Leif24
comments.text:
Wow, the UX kind of sucks. I just wanted to browse the devices - no way to do that, only option is an 'ai' search. Ok, I type in "Show All Devices" and it canoodles for about 30 seconds (great use of compute resources) only to apparently come up with the phrase "Browse all devices available in the directory without specific project-based filters." that it then seems to feed into the actual search tool... which then shows no results.

Two thoughts - (1) is your device list some state secret that you have to hide behind a search functionality? Because it would be nice to be able to browse through it without needing to engage in some sort of 'search'. (2) can you provide another option besides an ai search that is so intelligent that it has superseded the ability to do wildcard searches?

Edit: same feedback, but for the projects section as well.
comments.analysis: The user highlights legitimate usability issues with the AI search interface, providing constructive feedback about the need for browsing functionality.

comments.author: matheusmoreira
comments.text:
Please add the Sensor Watch to the list. It's an amazing project which created an open source replacement PCB and and even a custom LCD for the legendary Casio F-91W. Fully customizable open source firmware. It's got a temperature sensor which makes it a world class temperature compensated quartz watch.

I've contributed some work to it. Improved the pulsometer so it could also be used as an asthmometer which I really needed. Also improved the TOTP auth apps a bit. I was even one of the maintainers for a while.

It's an awesome project to hack on. Lots of nice people in the community. Highly recommended.
comments.analysis: This comment adds value by suggesting a relevant open hardware project and sharing firsthand experience, supporting the community aspect.

comments.author: dogman1050
comments.text: Having used only various ESP and RPi types, I found the article informative and the comments moreso.  AI slop or not, a list is a good place to start.
comments.analysis: Offers a balanced perspective appreciating the list's utility despite its flaws, which adds nuance to the discussion.

comments.author: voidUpdate
comments.text: How many of these are something that a normal consumer would buy, e.g. not a dev board that you can obviously flash your own code onto? I was sort of hoping for a list of things like "spotify car thing", "facebook portal" (I know neither of those are flashable yet, they've just been on my mind) and them maybe a list of projects for them or a link to a wiki page or something
comments.analysis: Raises a valid point about the target audience and product types featured, highlighting a potential gap in the directory's offerings.

comments.author: scientism
comments.text: Not commenting on the content itself (others have done it better) but anybody getting tired of seeing the same vibe coded websites over and over again?
comments.analysis: This comment is more of a superficial critique on aesthetics and does not contribute meaningfully to the discussion about the directory's content or functionality.

comments.author: cultureulterior
comments.text: All of your sensors are lies
comments.analysis: This vague and unsupported statement lacks context or evidence, making it unhelpful and likely untrue.
